text.title: Factory Boy Patterns
text.summary: Factory Boy vs model_bakery selection, SubFactory, pytest_factoryboy.register, and LazyAttribute for cycles.

rule.statement: Use Factory Boy for production test suites with complex model relationships. Use model_bakery only for rapid prototyping or tests where the exact field values do not matter.
rule.rationale: Factory Boy provides explicit, reusable factories with SubFactory chaining and Trait overrides. model_bakery auto-generates values but cannot express relationships with specific constraints. In large suites, model_bakery's implicit generation creates hard-to-debug flaky tests when generated values violate business rules.

rule.statement: Create exactly one Factory Boy factory per model and register it with `pytest_factoryboy.register`. Registrations go in `tests/conftest.py`. Factories live in `tests/factories.py`.
rule.rationale: Multiple factories per model lead to divergence — one factory creates a "valid" user but another creates a user with different defaults. A single factory with `Trait` variants for special cases is more maintainable. `pytest_factoryboy.register` auto-creates `user` and `user_factory` pytest fixtures from `UserFactory`.

example.note: Factory Boy factory with SubFactory and Traits
example.code:
import factory from factory.django import DjangoModelFactory from faker import Faker
fake = Faker()
class OrganizationFactory(DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = "organizations.Organization"
name = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: fake.company()) plan = "free"
class UserFactory(DjangoModelFactory): class Meta: model = "users.User"
email = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: fake.unique.email()) first_name = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: fake.first_name()) last_name = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: fake.last_name()) organization = factory.SubFactory(OrganizationFactory) is_active = True
class Params: staff = factory.Trait(is_staff=True, is_superuser=False) admin = factory.Trait(is_staff=True, is_superuser=True) inactive = factory.Trait(is_active=False)

rule.statement: Register all factories in `tests/conftest.py` using `pytest_factoryboy.register`. This auto-creates `model` (single instance) and `model_factory` (the factory class) fixtures for every registered factory.
rule.rationale: `register(UserFactory)` creates `user` and `user_factory` pytest fixtures automatically. Tests can receive `user` for a pre-created instance or `user_factory` to create customized instances. This eliminates boilerplate fixture definitions for every model variant.

example.code:
# tests/conftest.py from pytest_factoryboy import register from tests.factories import UserFactory, OrganizationFactory, OrderFactory
register(OrganizationFactory) # Provides: organization, organization_factory register(UserFactory) # Provides: user, user_factory register(OrderFactory) # Provides: order, order_factory

rule.statement: Use `factory.LazyAttribute(lambda o: ...)` to derive field values from other fields on the same factory. Use `factory.LazyFunction(lambda: ...)` for independent computed values (unique emails, timestamps).
rule.rationale: `LazyAttribute` receives the object being constructed as `o`, allowing derived values like `username = LazyAttribute(lambda o: o.email.split('@')[0])`. `LazyFunction` is for values that don't depend on other fields but must be computed at factory call time (not at class definition time).

example.note: LazyAttribute for derived username from email
